titleStmt.title: The Acting Secretary of the Navy to the Secretary of State..

div.head: The Acting Secretary of the Navy to the Secretary of State .
div.p: Sir: I have the honor to transmit herewith, for the information of the Department of State, a copy of a report, dated October 18th, 1912, received from the Commander-in-Chief, Pacific Fleet, at Corinto, concerning operations in Nicaragua.

frus:attachment.head: The Commander-in-Chief, Pacific Fleet, to the Secretary of the Navy .

opener.dateline:
U. S. S. Annapolis,
Corinto , October 18, 1912 .

frus:attachment.p: The conditions in Western Nicaragua have steadily improved since the date of my last report and, due to the excellent results of our practical occupation of the late rebel towns from León to Chinandega both inclusive, the intensely bitter feeling existing between the two national parties is gradually quieting down and in addition, a desire for permanent peaceful conditions is steadily growing amongst the better classes.

The greater part of the Government army has been disbanded. This fact, with my order that no Government troops should be permitted to come within two miles of any of the towns referred to, and the knowledge that no reprisals of any kind would be permitted in any town occupied by our bluejackets and marines, has convinced the inhabitants that we are here, not to seize their country, but simply in the cause of humanity and to restore law and order. This belief has possibly been accentuated by the fact that I have extended the Department’s order to protect Americans and other foreigners and their property to include all reputable Nicaraguans living in the towns above referred to, this action being necessary and wise under the existing circumstances.

All of the Government officials have been installed at León and the wheels of Government are now beginning to run smoothly, these officials all cooperating in the heartiest manner with Lieutenant Colonel Long to restore law and order and regain the confidence of the inhabitants. Stores and private houses have been opened and the city has nearly resumed its normal condition and appearance. Through the cooperation of some of the officials and prominent residents, Lieutenant Colonel Long has been able to obtain quite a number of rifles and dynamite bombs, and also a considerable quantity of Government tobacco which had been previously seized by the late rebels.

In Chinandega and the intermediate towns a similar improvement has taken place. In the first named town P. A. Surgeon C. B. Munger, U. S. Navy, has been made sanitary officer and the improved sanitary conditions resulting from his work are very noticeable. The same is also the case in Corinto where Assistant Surgeon C. M. George, U. S. Navy, has been acting as sanitary officer for some time.

On October 16th, Commander D. W. Blamer of the Cleveland, stationed at San Juan del Sur, sent a small party of mounted bluejackets under command
of Lieutenant M. S. Davis, U. S. Navy, across country to Virgin Bay on Lake Nicaragua, overdue old Vanderbilt transit road which was used by the California gold seekers in the early fifties, and which has been but little used of late years. This being the rainy season the road was very bad, mud in places being three feet deep. The distance is about ten miles but it took fourteen hours for the round trip. The road passes through a rich agricultural country the inhabitants of which welcomed and entertained the party and made it clear that they were tired of revolutions and wanted to work their farms and enjoy the blessings of peace. An intelligence report will be forwarded later.

The conditions now being favorable I have informed the manager of the Corinto-Granada railroad that, if he does not need our assistance hereafter, I will direct the removal of all of our people now connected with the railroad except a small armed guard for each passenger train.
The guard of one company of marines now stationed at Granada will be reduced one half, and that of one section of marines now stationed at Masaya will be removed entirely, within one month.

The landing parties of the Cleveland and Denver will be withdrawn within one week, while those of the California, Colorado and Annapolis will be withdrawn, a few at a time, in such a manner as to have them all on board ship by or before November 15th, those at Corinto and San Juan del Sur remaining until the last.

The third battalion of marines will then have headquarters at Managua, one section of a company of which will be temporarily stationed at Granada. The first and second battalions of marines will have headquarters at León, one section of a company being temporarily stationed at Chinandega and two squads each at Chichigalpa, Posoltega and Quezalguaque.

From the best information obtainable it appears that the return fire of the third battalion of marines and the California and Annapolis bluejackets when Major Butler’s train was attacked in passing through Masaya on the night of September 19th and four marines were wounded, resulted in the killing of fifty-six rebels and the wounding of seventy others, of whom twelve died in the hospital later. It also appears that the rebels attack was premeditated, and thus an act of treachery on the part of General Zeledón.

The Department, of course, understands that I now have eyes and ears in each of the important towns in Western Nicaragua except Matagalpa; these eyes and ears being of Navy and Marine officers who know how to mix with and make themselves agreeable to people of all classes. In a few days I expect to have a set of bright, intelligent Navy and Marine officers in Matagalpa to observe and report upon the actual conditions in and about that town.
The accompanying letter, with translation, sent to me by the ladies of the city of Granada, may prove of interest to the Department. An appropriate response has been made.

frus:attachment.head: Ladies of Granada to Admiral W. H. H. Southerland .
frus:attachment.p: Honorable Sir: Daughters of this beloved soil which God has endowed with a prodigality of beauty and natural riches, we should feel happy and contented if we had the tranquility indispensible for the development and well-being of the family; but, unfortunately, civil conflicts have resulted in weakening the ties which in every civilized country are considered sacred and which maintain the stability of all well organized society, which cannot exist without due respect for life and property.

These conflicts have been each day more stubborn and bloody, the bitterness each day greater, and the loss each time more irreparable, bringing about a division so deep in the Nicaraguan family that the most perverse elements predominated in this city and it fell to our lot to live in a state of horror and fright in which the mother, the daughter, the wife, and the sister saw themselves each moment threatened with the loss of all they held most dear, of their subsistence, of the most necessary things even for the life of the children, with the loss of their own honor.

It is for this, seeing the prolongation of the recent conflict which was growing fiercer each day, that we celebrated with enthusiasm your arrival on
the shores of Nicaragua as an omen that soon our ills would cease. Our hopes were fulfilled, inasmuch as you have given with wonderful rapidity the peace and tranquility that we so longed for.

On account of the shortness of your stay in this city, we were not able to express to you personally our gratitude and profound esteem, so we send you a bouquet of flowers from our gardens, which in their perfume carry for you and your great and generous country the tenderest feelings of the women of Granada.

The judgment and prudence with which your worthy subordinate officer, Major S. D. Butler, U. S. M. C., has executed his mission in this city gives credit to the high efficiency of the officers and the morale and discipline of the American forces.

Already we have enjoyed for these few days real peace and tranquility with the stay of the American forces in our midst. It is for us to express the desire that our elder sister, the great Republic of the United States, so wise, so powerful, will bring to us permanently the benefits which all her sons enjoy throughout all her vast and peaceful domain.
